The gardens were created in 1807 by order of Napoleon Bonaparte and designed by Gianantonio Selva as a public green space for the citizens of Venice. Today, they serve both as a peaceful retreat and as a major cultural venue. Since 1895, the gardens have hosted the Venice Biennale, beginning with its first International Art Exhibition. During the Biennale, approximately 65% of the gardens are dedicated to exhibition space, while the remaining 35% remains open as public parkland. Starting in 1907, individual nations began constructing their own pavilions within the gardens. Today, there are 29 national pavilions, making the gardens one of the central hubs for contemporary art during the Biennale.

The bienalle for architecture is both mind blowing and overwhelming. The 2025 installation was very focused on climate change, global growth and usage of resources and how materials/technologies are used for global problem solving. It is important to go in with an open mind and time to process the information. Some countries and exhibits are better than others in terms of interpretation and information. For the year 2025, the beauty is in the details. The exhibition u Is not about architecture as many think of it, but rather about elements of architecture. And, if you are not comfortable with the English or Italian language, you will likely be lost and confused. It is wise to get the three-day pass and break up your time to allow the ability to process the density of information. There are many restaurants and cafes in the Giardini but in the main bienalle exhibit in the arsenale area there is only one large cafe and restaurant. Be sure to download area maps before you explore to get a good sense of place and understand the exhibits before you explore. The water taxis will bring you to the main stops for arsenale and giardini.

Beautiful location for the biennial art and architecture Biennales. At the Giardini are most of the national pavilions. At the 2025 Architecture Biennale I was particularly impressed by the US and Scandinavian pavilions with the former focusing on the porch in US architecture with great examples like Ocean Grove, New Jersey and Chautauqua, New York. The Greek pavilion was impressive as well with a focus on the two Venetian shipyards on the island of Crete. The Israeli pavilion was closed for renovation as was the pavilion of Venezuela, designed by Carlo Scarpa (1906-1978) who also designed the original entrance pavilion to the Biennale.

Amid the bustling canals and throngs of visitors that define Venice, the Giardini della Biennale offers a breath of calm. This leafy enclave, tucked along the Bacino di San Marco, isn’t a destination for grand botanical wonders, but its quiet charm and historical roots make it a worthy detour for those seeking respite.

Originally marshland transformed by Napoleon into public gardens, the space now serves dual purposes: a peaceful retreat and a cultural hub. Its winding paths lead visitors past 30 pavilions, each representing a different nation and showcasing architectural flair from icons like Carlo Scarpa and Alvar Aalto. Though the structures truly come alive during the Venice Biennale Art Festival—when global creativity takes center stage—they retain a quiet magnetism year-round.

The gardens’ unpretentious beauty is amplified by quirky details: feral cats lounging in sunlit corners, the stoic gaze of Garibaldi’s statue at the entrance, and a serene coastal walk that skirts the water’s edge. These elements stitch together a patchwork of tranquility, a stark contrast to the nearby chaos of St. Mark’s Square.

Yet, for all its understated appeal, the Giardini della Biennale remains a “nice-to-have” rather than a must-see. If your Venice itinerary is packed, you won’t miss a life-changing experience by skipping it. But for those with moments to spare, it’s a gentle reminder that even in a city overrun by tourism, pockets of stillness—and a few curious cats—still endure.

Stumbled upon Giardini Reali just behind Piazza San Marco, and it instantly became my go-to oasis in the city. With shaded benches, pergola-covered walkways, and a lovely view of the lagoon, it’s the perfect spot to recharge between sightseeing bursts  .

Unexpected calm: Even on busy days, the garden’s tucked-away location makes it feel almost secret—perfect for a pause with a book or a snack  .

Lovely design touches: A restored 19th-century greenhouse, drawbridge to Piazza San Marco, and the longest wisteria pergola in Venice give it both charm and elegance  .

Free and easy access: Open to the public, and ideal for hanging out while you wait for the vaporetto at Giardinetti stop. No tickets, no rush  .

Tucked away just around the corner from the bustling St. Mark’s Square, the Gardens of the Venice Foundation (Giardini della Fondazione Venezia) are a true hidden gem—a quiet haven of greenery and tranquility in a city better known for its canals and narrow alleys. We stumbled upon this secret spot during our visit and ended up spending a wonderfully peaceful hour here, simply relaxing with an ice cream and enjoying the natural beauty.

The gardens have an interesting history, originally part of the private grounds of a noble Venetian palace. Today, they are one of the few green spaces in the historic center, offering a rare escape from the crowds. The well-maintained paths wind through a delightful mix of Mediterranean plants, shady trees, and blooming flowers, creating an atmosphere that feels worlds away from the nearby tourist hotspots.

One of the most enjoyable aspects was the sense of seclusion—even though we were only minutes from the heart of Venice, the gardens felt almost undiscovered. The benches and quiet corners make it an ideal spot to pause, unwind, and soak in the beauty of the surroundings.

For anyone looking for a moment of calm in the midst of Venice’s energy, this is the perfect place. Whether you’re escaping the midday heat, looking for a quiet place to read, or simply wanting to appreciate another side of the city, the Gardens of the Venice Foundation are well worth seeking out.

The Giardini Reali in Venice, also known as the Royal Gardens, offer a serene escape from the bustling streets of the city. These historic gardens, originally created for royalty, boast lush greenery, vibrant flowers, and tranquil pathways perfect for leisurely strolls. Visitors can admire the elegant statues and fountains scattered throughout the park, while enjoying panoramic views of the Venetian lagoon. Whether relaxing on a bench or exploring the hidden corners, the Giardini Reali provide a peaceful retreat and a glimpse into Venice's regal past.

The park in Italy was simple but beautiful, with wide stretches of bright green grass that looked almost painted under the sunlight. It was the kind of place where everything felt calm and open. The grass was soft and freshly cut, carrying that clean, earthy smell that comes after a morning watering. People sat on blankets or benches, talking quietly or just watching the day go by.
Children ran across the open field, their shoes kicking up tiny bits of grass as they played, and every so often a light breeze would move through, making the leaves in the nearby trees rustle softly. There were a few old stone paths that curved through the park, leading to shaded spots where the grass grew a little taller and wilder.
From one end, you could see the red roofs of nearby houses and hear the faint sounds of a café — clinking cups, laughter, and the hiss of an espresso machine. It wasn’t a large park, but it had a peaceful feeling, like it belonged to the people who used it every day. The green grass stretched out like a soft carpet, inviting anyone to sit, rest, or just enjoy the view. It was simple, sunny, and perfectly Italian in its quiet charm

I asked in confusion why the very large dogs were in the small dog area and I was told it didn't matter, and yet the pictures told a different story. Why have any picture if they mean nothing. Parco di Villa Groggia is a serene, lesser-known park in Venice's Cannaregio district, offering a peaceful retreat from the city's bustle. Visitors can enjoy shaded paths, benches, and the charming Teatrino Groggia, a small 19th-century theater hosting community events .

Historically, the park occupies the site of the 16th-century Palazzo Donà, demolished in 1823. In the late 19th century, Giuseppe Groggia developed the area into a romantic-style garden. After his death, the property was bequeathed to the San Gioacchino Institute in Mestre for the education of underprivileged girls. Since 1975, the City of Venice has owned and maintained the park .

Today, Parco di Villa Groggia serves as a tranquil space for locals and visitors alike, featuring shaded areas, benches, and a community center, making it an ideal spot to relax and experience a quieter side of Venice.

Beautiful area with lots of green space, a park in the heart of Mestre and just across from Venice. The perfect place for complete rest and relaxation. Inside the park are great restaurants and bars that offer good food and drink. The green area offers a good view of Venice and the Venetian lagoon. Very open, with lots of trails for both runners and walkers and cyclists. Everything is great done. It is easy to walk or run 4-5 km around the park, the lagoon and its trails. Apart from being an area for environmental and landscape recovery, it is also one of the most important places to study the lagoon environment, it is also popular as a destination for sports and cultural activities for leisure. An ideal place to stay in nature with the family. Created thanks to the European contribution, San Giuliano Park is an ambitious project, one of the most significant urban conversion and land reclamation interventions in Italy due to its complexity and articulation, as well as its dimensional and territorial characteristics. San Giuliano Park is one of the richest areas of biodiversity in the Venetian lagoon environment, and is known for being an excellent area for bird watching to visit the lagoon, attracting the interest of many nature photographers and observers, especially ornithologists. The park is actually known to enthusiasts for its rich avifauna, as it is a nesting area for many species of birds.
Want to go on a picnic? Want to go for a run, feel like jogging or even just to get fresh air? This is your place. No matter the season, you’ll love it.

Parco di Villa Groggia is a serene, lesser-known park in Venice's Cannaregio district, offering a peaceful retreat from the city's bustle. Visitors can enjoy shaded paths, benches, and the charming Teatrino Groggia, a small 19th-century theater hosting community events .

Historically, the park occupies the site of the 16th-century Palazzo Donà, demolished in 1823. In the late 19th century, Giuseppe Groggia developed the area into a romantic-style garden. After his death, the property was bequeathed to the San Gioacchino Institute in Mestre for the education of underprivileged girls. Since 1975, the City of Venice has owned and maintained the park .

Today, Parco di Villa Groggia serves as a tranquil space for locals and visitors alike, featuring shaded areas, benches, and a community center, making it an ideal spot to relax and experience a quieter side of Venice.

Situata davanti al porto di San Nicolò di Lido, uno dei tre varchi che mette in comunicazione la laguna con il mare Adriatico. Nel corso del XVI secolo, la Repubblica di Venezia fece erigere una moderna fortezza per difendersi da eventuali attacchi dal mare. In precedenza, il sito ospitava un castello posto a guardia di una robusta e lunga catena che sbarrava l'accesso alle navi nemiche. La fortezza venne ideata dal capitano generale dell' esercito veneziano, il duca di Urbino Francesco Maria I della Rovere, e progettata dell'architetto Michele Sanmicheli che mascherò la moderna macchina militare, dotata di artiglierie per sparare a pelo d'acqua, con una struttura architettonica di ordine rustico, ad imitazione degli antichi archi di trionfo romani. Il luogo meriterebbe una più degna valorizzazione sia del monumento che della natura che lo circonda, da aprire al pubblico e da mettere in relazione con l'antistante fortezza di San Nicolò, ora abbandonata e un tempo sede del corpo dei Lagunari, annessi alla divisione Folgore.

Parco di Villa Groggia is a serene, lesser-known park in Venice's Cannaregio district, offering a peaceful retreat from the city's bustle. Visitors can enjoy shaded paths, benches, and the charming Teatrino Groggia, a small 19th-century theater hosting community events .

Historically, the park occupies the site of the 16th-century Palazzo Donà, demolished in 1823. In the late 19th century, Giuseppe Groggia developed the area into a romantic-style garden. After his death, the property was bequeathed to the San Gioacchino Institute in Mestre for the education of underprivileged girls. Since 1975, the City of Venice has owned and maintained the park .

Today, Parco di Villa Groggia serves as a tranquil space for locals and visitors alike, featuring shaded areas, benches, and a community center, making it an ideal spot to relax and experience a quieter side of Venice.

The bienalle for architecture is both mind blowing and overwhelming. The 2025 installation was very focused on climate change, global growth and usage of resources and how materials/technologies are used for global problem solving. It is important to go in with an open mind and time to process the information. Some countries and exhibits are better than others in terms of interpretation and information. For the year 2025, the beauty is in the details. The exhibition u Is not about architecture as many think of it, but rather about elements of architecture. And, if you are not comfortable with the English or Italian language, you will likely be lost and confused. It is wise to get the three-day pass and break up your time to allow the ability to process the density of information. There are many restaurants and cafes in the Giardini but in the main bienalle exhibit in the arsenale area there is only one large cafe and restaurant. Be sure to download area maps before you explore to get a good sense of place and understand the exhibits before you explore. The water taxis will bring you to the main stops for arsenale and giardini.

Beautiful location for the biennial art and architecture Biennales. At the Giardini are most of the national pavilions. At the 2025 Architecture Biennale I was particularly impressed by the US and Scandinavian pavilions with the former focusing on the porch in US architecture with great examples like Ocean Grove, New Jersey and Chautauqua, New York. The Greek pavilion was impressive as well with a focus on the two Venetian shipyards on the island of Crete. The Israeli pavilion was closed for renovation as was the pavilion of Venezuela, designed by Carlo Scarpa (1906-1978) who also designed the original entrance pavilion to the Biennale.

Amid the bustling canals and throngs of visitors that define Venice, the Giardini della Biennale offers a breath of calm. This leafy enclave, tucked along the Bacino di San Marco, isn’t a destination for grand botanical wonders, but its quiet charm and historical roots make it a worthy detour for those seeking respite.

Originally marshland transformed by Napoleon into public gardens, the space now serves dual purposes: a peaceful retreat and a cultural hub. Its winding paths lead visitors past 30 pavilions, each representing a different nation and showcasing architectural flair from icons like Carlo Scarpa and Alvar Aalto. Though the structures truly come alive during the Venice Biennale Art Festival—when global creativity takes center stage—they retain a quiet magnetism year-round.

The gardens’ unpretentious beauty is amplified by quirky details: feral cats lounging in sunlit corners, the stoic gaze of Garibaldi’s statue at the entrance, and a serene coastal walk that skirts the water’s edge. These elements stitch together a patchwork of tranquility, a stark contrast to the nearby chaos of St. Mark’s Square.

Yet, for all its understated appeal, the Giardini della Biennale remains a “nice-to-have” rather than a must-see. If your Venice itinerary is packed, you won’t miss a life-changing experience by skipping it. But for those with moments to spare, it’s a gentle reminder that even in a city overrun by tourism, pockets of stillness—and a few curious cats—still endure.

The gardens were created in 1807 by order of Napoleon Bonaparte and designed by Gianantonio Selva as a public green space for the citizens of Venice. Today, they serve both as a peaceful retreat and as a major cultural venue. Since 1895, the gardens have hosted the Venice Biennale, beginning with its first International Art Exhibition. During the Biennale, approximately 65% of the gardens are dedicated to exhibition space, while the remaining 35% remains open as public parkland. Starting in 1907, individual nations began constructing their own pavilions within the gardens. Today, there are 29 national pavilions, making the gardens one of the central hubs for contemporary art during the Biennale.

The Giardini Reali is a nice surprise located right next to Piazza San Marco. It's a small garden, but beautiful and cozy! Perfect for a quick break, away from the crowds. The landscaping is well-done, and it's a great place to sit, breathe, and enjoy nature.

This was my second time in Venice, and visiting the Giardini Reali truly felt like a refreshing escape in the heart of the city.

I spent almost two hours here, simply enjoying the flowers, their scents, the peaceful walkways, and the calm beauty of it all.
It’s a quiet, green corner filled with life, color, and charm, a wonderful experience on its own.

Whether you're a nature lover or just need a break from the busy streets of Venice, this garden is an absolute gem worth visiting. ♥️

Stumbled upon Giardini Reali just behind Piazza San Marco, and it instantly became my go-to oasis in the city. With shaded benches, pergola-covered walkways, and a lovely view of the lagoon, it’s the perfect spot to recharge between sightseeing bursts  .

Unexpected calm: Even on busy days, the garden’s tucked-away location makes it feel almost secret—perfect for a pause with a book or a snack  .

Lovely design touches: A restored 19th-century greenhouse, drawbridge to Piazza San Marco, and the longest wisteria pergola in Venice give it both charm and elegance  .

Free and easy access: Open to the public, and ideal for hanging out while you wait for the vaporetto at Giardinetti stop. No tickets, no rush  .

Tucked away just around the corner from the bustling St. Mark’s Square, the Gardens of the Venice Foundation (Giardini della Fondazione Venezia) are a true hidden gem—a quiet haven of greenery and tranquility in a city better known for its canals and narrow alleys. We stumbled upon this secret spot during our visit and ended up spending a wonderfully peaceful hour here, simply relaxing with an ice cream and enjoying the natural beauty.

The gardens have an interesting history, originally part of the private grounds of a noble Venetian palace. Today, they are one of the few green spaces in the historic center, offering a rare escape from the crowds. The well-maintained paths wind through a delightful mix of Mediterranean plants, shady trees, and blooming flowers, creating an atmosphere that feels worlds away from the nearby tourist hotspots.

One of the most enjoyable aspects was the sense of seclusion—even though we were only minutes from the heart of Venice, the gardens felt almost undiscovered. The benches and quiet corners make it an ideal spot to pause, unwind, and soak in the beauty of the surroundings.

For anyone looking for a moment of calm in the midst of Venice’s energy, this is the perfect place. Whether you’re escaping the midday heat, looking for a quiet place to read, or simply wanting to appreciate another side of the city, the Gardens of the Venice Foundation are well worth seeking out.

The park in Italy was simple but beautiful, with wide stretches of bright green grass that looked almost painted under the sunlight. It was the kind of place where everything felt calm and open. The grass was soft and freshly cut, carrying that clean, earthy smell that comes after a morning watering. People sat on blankets or benches, talking quietly or just watching the day go by.
Children ran across the open field, their shoes kicking up tiny bits of grass as they played, and every so often a light breeze would move through, making the leaves in the nearby trees rustle softly. There were a few old stone paths that curved through the park, leading to shaded spots where the grass grew a little taller and wilder.
From one end, you could see the red roofs of nearby houses and hear the faint sounds of a café — clinking cups, laughter, and the hiss of an espresso machine. It wasn’t a large park, but it had a peaceful feeling, like it belonged to the people who used it every day. The green grass stretched out like a soft carpet, inviting anyone to sit, rest, or just enjoy the view. It was simple, sunny, and perfectly Italian in its quiet charm

This was a beautiful experience. It was lightly raining, with sun, while we were there which added to the beauty!

We would love to experience it in another season, and will probably go back! Thank you!

Beautiful area with lots of green space, a park in the heart of Mestre and just across from Venice. The perfect place for complete rest and relaxation. Inside the park are great restaurants and bars that offer good food and drink. The green area offers a good view of Venice and the Venetian lagoon. Very open, with lots of trails for both runners and walkers and cyclists. Everything is great done. It is easy to walk or run 4-5 km around the park, the lagoon and its trails. Apart from being an area for environmental and landscape recovery, it is also one of the most important places to study the lagoon environment, it is also popular as a destination for sports and cultural activities for leisure. An ideal place to stay in nature with the family. Created thanks to the European contribution, San Giuliano Park is an ambitious project, one of the most significant urban conversion and land reclamation interventions in Italy due to its complexity and articulation, as well as its dimensional and territorial characteristics. San Giuliano Park is one of the richest areas of biodiversity in the Venetian lagoon environment, and is known for being an excellent area for bird watching to visit the lagoon, attracting the interest of many nature photographers and observers, especially ornithologists. The park is actually known to enthusiasts for its rich avifauna, as it is a nesting area for many species of birds.
Want to go on a picnic? Want to go for a run, feel like jogging or even just to get fresh air? This is your place. No matter the season, you’ll love it.

Upon arrival this morning at 7.15 am from Croatia, the Harbour Master was awaiting for us to guide us to our berth and helped us secure the boat. Il parco Emmer, come avrebbero voluto da sempre abitanti e frequentatori, sta lentamente diventando una delle aree verdi più apprezzate del quartiere. Il parco si trova in Via P.Cornaglia a poca distanza dal Centro Commerciale Panorama. Il bellissimo parco offre un posto meraviglioso per rilassarsi, fare jogging, fare una piacevole passeggiata, divertirsi con i bambini o giocare a calcetto. This is a small but very refined park in the center of Mestre. Villa Querini has a formal garden at the front and a more informal, romantically planted area at the back. The park has benches, a children's playground, a herb garden, an enclosed dog park and bike lanes. The park isn't too big so it's easier to keep track of your kids. The Marzenego River flows along the park and it just makes the whole place shine! This park was one of the only ones in the city before the city administrations decided to plant more trees and parks. I certainly suggest you visit the place if happen to be in Mestre.

Parco Albanese di Bissuola interessa un’area di 33 ettari di cui 21 ettari di impianti prativi restante 12 ettari di aree a bosco e superfici pavimentate (piazzali, vialetti, superfici da gioco ecc.) e superfici acquee.

Le aree del Parco Albanese di Bissuola comprendono: aree verdi a prato ed arbustive, aree laghetto e oasi ornitologica, giochi d’acqua, percorsi pedonali e ciclabili, impianti sportivi e ricreativi (campetti da calcio, pallavolo, basket, tennis, bocce, pattinaggio, piscina, bar e ristorante, area giochi attrezzata) parcheggi per auto e motocicli. All’interno del Parco si trovano anche il Teatro del Parco, la Biblioteca di quartiere, l’Orto Botanico Locatelli, annesso agli orti comunali. Nelle Zone prative ed arbustive, le specie botaniche più rappresentate sono quelle tipiche dei boschi della pianura padano/veneta, quali Populus nigra, Carpinus betulus, Qercus pedunculata; fraxinus oxicarpa, Acer pseudoplatanus, Tilia cordata, e Tilia platyphillos, Salix alba, Alnus glutinosa ed alcune specie “esotiche” Salisburia adiantifolia (Gingko biloba), Celtis australis, Abies cephalonica e Cedrus deodara; In totale gli alberi sono 1.370. Circa 1300 le piante di rose, rose Multiflora e Mutabilis che fiancheggiano il lato NO dello stagno palmipedi e rampicanti che coprono il pergolato dell’isola rialzata in mattoni facciavista .

The bienalle for architecture is both mind blowing and overwhelming. The 2025 installation was very focused on climate change, global growth and usage of resources and how materials/technologies are used for global problem solving. It is important to go in with an open mind and time to process the information. Some countries and exhibits are better than others in terms of interpretation and information. For the year 2025, the beauty is in the details. The exhibition u Is not about architecture as many think of it, but rather about elements of architecture. And, if you are not comfortable with the English or Italian language, you will likely be lost and confused. It is wise to get the three-day pass and break up your time to allow the ability to process the density of information. There are many restaurants and cafes in the Giardini but in the main bienalle exhibit in the arsenale area there is only one large cafe and restaurant. Be sure to download area maps before you explore to get a good sense of place and understand the exhibits before you explore. The water taxis will bring you to the main stops for arsenale and giardini.

Amid the bustling canals and throngs of visitors that define Venice, the Giardini della Biennale offers a breath of calm. This leafy enclave, tucked along the Bacino di San Marco, isn’t a destination for grand botanical wonders, but its quiet charm and historical roots make it a worthy detour for those seeking respite.

Originally marshland transformed by Napoleon into public gardens, the space now serves dual purposes: a peaceful retreat and a cultural hub. Its winding paths lead visitors past 30 pavilions, each representing a different nation and showcasing architectural flair from icons like Carlo Scarpa and Alvar Aalto. Though the structures truly come alive during the Venice Biennale Art Festival—when global creativity takes center stage—they retain a quiet magnetism year-round.

The gardens’ unpretentious beauty is amplified by quirky details: feral cats lounging in sunlit corners, the stoic gaze of Garibaldi’s statue at the entrance, and a serene coastal walk that skirts the water’s edge. These elements stitch together a patchwork of tranquility, a stark contrast to the nearby chaos of St. Mark’s Square.

Yet, for all its understated appeal, the Giardini della Biennale remains a “nice-to-have” rather than a must-see. If your Venice itinerary is packed, you won’t miss a life-changing experience by skipping it. But for those with moments to spare, it’s a gentle reminder that even in a city overrun by tourism, pockets of stillness—and a few curious cats—still endure.

Tucked away just around the corner from the bustling St. Mark’s Square, the Gardens of the Venice Foundation (Giardini della Fondazione Venezia) are a true hidden gem—a quiet haven of greenery and tranquility in a city better known for its canals and narrow alleys. We stumbled upon this secret spot during our visit and ended up spending a wonderfully peaceful hour here, simply relaxing with an ice cream and enjoying the natural beauty.

The gardens have an interesting history, originally part of the private grounds of a noble Venetian palace. Today, they are one of the few green spaces in the historic center, offering a rare escape from the crowds. The well-maintained paths wind through a delightful mix of Mediterranean plants, shady trees, and blooming flowers, creating an atmosphere that feels worlds away from the nearby tourist hotspots.

One of the most enjoyable aspects was the sense of seclusion—even though we were only minutes from the heart of Venice, the gardens felt almost undiscovered. The benches and quiet corners make it an ideal spot to pause, unwind, and soak in the beauty of the surroundings.

For anyone looking for a moment of calm in the midst of Venice’s energy, this is the perfect place. Whether you’re escaping the midday heat, looking for a quiet place to read, or simply wanting to appreciate another side of the city, the Gardens of the Venice Foundation are well worth seeking out.

The gardens were created in 1807 by order of Napoleon Bonaparte and designed by Gianantonio Selva as a public green space for the citizens of Venice. Today, they serve both as a peaceful retreat and as a major cultural venue. Since 1895, the gardens have hosted the Venice Biennale, beginning with its first International Art Exhibition. During the Biennale, approximately 65% of the gardens are dedicated to exhibition space, while the remaining 35% remains open as public parkland. Starting in 1907, individual nations began constructing their own pavilions within the gardens. Today, there are 29 national pavilions, making the gardens one of the central hubs for contemporary art during the Biennale.

Parco di Villa Groggia is a serene, lesser-known park in Venice's Cannaregio district, offering a peaceful retreat from the city's bustle. Visitors can enjoy shaded paths, benches, and the charming Teatrino Groggia, a small 19th-century theater hosting community events .

Historically, the park occupies the site of the 16th-century Palazzo Donà, demolished in 1823. In the late 19th century, Giuseppe Groggia developed the area into a romantic-style garden. After his death, the property was bequeathed to the San Gioacchino Institute in Mestre for the education of underprivileged girls. Since 1975, the City of Venice has owned and maintained the park .

Today, Parco di Villa Groggia serves as a tranquil space for locals and visitors alike, featuring shaded areas, benches, and a community center, making it an ideal spot to relax and experience a quieter side of Venice.

Visiting this place felt like finally taking a deep breath after a long day in the crowded center of Venice. We found an oasis we wouldn’t have dared to hope for.

We were surrounded by greenery whose energy felt almost healing: oleanders in full bloom, and the tallest ficus we have ever seen, casting deep, generous shade that became a sanctuary from the Venetian sun. Beneath its branches, the children discovered a little play kitchen set in a natural, thoughtful setting just for them. It wasn’t just a token gesture – you could feel that the owners, being parents themselves, understand what children truly need: a safe corner, room for imagination, and the simple joy of play in nature.

We discovered flavors that are so often lost under the weight of tourist compromises – tender octopus with lime cream, sweet fresh peas, roasted cauliflower, mussels, and fish so fresh it still carried the scent of the sea.

Thank you for the piano music that tied it all together into one breathtaking experience.

Osteria Al Giardino is Venice in miniature: charming on the surface, built for tourists, and just authentic enough to keep the illusion intact. It’s the culinary equivalent of a gondola ride with a Bluetooth speaker—technically correct, emotionally vacant.

The food is passable. Pasta arrives al dente, sauces play it safe, and pizza is decent if unremarkable. Nothing offends, but nothing sticks with you either. It’s a menu engineered for first-time visitors who want Italian food with training wheels—familiar, friendly, and free of surprises.

The wine list is a joke. Just grape names scattered like keywords—Merlot, Chardonnay, and Rosé (helpfully listed as if it were a grape). No producers, no vintages, no mention of origin. It’s wine stripped of all the things that make it Italian.

And then there was the waiter, who, with unearned confidence, tried to explain that Pinot Bianco and Pinot Grigio are the same thing. I wanted to believe it was a language barrier. But no—he doubled down, blending misinformation with the same smooth assurance used to upsell dessert. He also gave me a muddled lesson on the difference between trattoria, osteria, and ristorante—and got that wrong too.

Service was fast but mechanical, like they’ve done this dance a thousand times before. Because they have.

This isn’t a bad place. It’s a safe place. A place for those on their first trip to Italy who want the postcard, not the novel. If that’s your vibe, you’ll leave satisfied. But if you’ve ever had real food, poured your own glass from the producer’s jug, or argued over pasta textures with a nonna—this will feel like a performance. And not a very convincing one.

The food, wine and atmosphere were all amazing! We got the mozzarella, melon and pruschitto appetizer which was wonderful and to eat we got the carbonara and fish soup which was the best we’ve ever had. If you go to Venice, you need to check it out! To end the lunch, we were also given a small glass of limoncello.
Shaded green area. If you're travelling with kids, it has a few prices of playground equipment that can make their time more enjoyable.
Beautiful Park with lots of seating and a playground.
A nice well hidden playground in the garden of the impressive gallery
The garden is very cozy and tranquil, but we went there specifically for the kids playground that, unfortunately, is temporarily closed
Fun place to take kids to use up some energy.
Nice hidden gem with a playground.

An amazing spot of serenity in an otherwise bustling city, this FREE garden is a great place to relax in the shade and take a break as you plan your next move. They also have a beautiful water fountain to refill your bottle with and stay hydrated.
Cute, relaxing garden, nothing spectacular

If you want to find a quiet restaurant in crowded Venice, this is a wonderful choice for you. Professional service, very helpful and friendly staffs, when I told the waiter that I will order a dish for share, the chef divided the dish in two plate which I dont usually see at fine dinning restaurants. If you come here you should try beverage at the bar as well, coke are served with free chips and chocolate with free biscuits are also a highlight for this bar

Was very disappointed in this restaurant. Had looked at reviews prior to coming and it looked like quite a lovely restaurant. My partner and I were both disappointed in our meals, mine in particular. I ordered the veal which was one of the most expensive items on their menu at €35. I honestly could have had a better meal from McDonald’s. Very over cooked and no flavour with a sauce that tasted like American hickory bbq? I sent almost the entire meal back which they kindly took off our bill, but still very disappointing.

This is actually a gem of a restaurant. The food is delightful, even the oil and balsamic vinegar they provide with their bread is exceptional. I wasn't sure what to expect given it is a hotel restaurant, but give it a go as it it is worth it!

this place is magical
the food and excellent
and the service is outstanding
is a must to come here if you stay in this hotel

Hotel is a way from main attractions, but nice park Papadopoulou behind, hence the name.   Would have liked to be in main restaurant, was in the bar(not very busy)But food was really good, and better because of nice discount on fork. Servers were also very nice!

This is probably the most beautiful restaurant in Venice however there is a reason I have regrettably only rated 3 stars...

The food is overpriced and some items are questionable.

For my first course I had an egg dish which ended up being a poached egg is what I can only describe as vegetable soup. I do not understand why you would spoil a runny poached egg by putting it into a soup. The flavour is lost.

For my second course I had a spicy lamb dish. It was not spicy and here it goes, the reason why this is not a fine dining restaurant, THEY SERVED THE LAMB WELL DONE! I asked the waiter if it should be rare and he said no! Does anybody know of a fine dining restaurant that would serve lamb anywhere over medium!?!

Overall lovely setting, average service, overpriced mediocre food.

Was a surprise for my girlfriends birthday, left feeling very embarrassed. Worst experience, overpriced, limited options and they overcharged me at the end. Forced us to order extra food, undercooked dessert. Drinks were full of ice. Please don’t bother coming here! Booked through the FORK app, refused to apply discount. Doesn’t deserve any rating. Terribile esperienza mortificato,

Went way above and beyond our expectations, we visited for my best friends hen do, the food was amazing, the service fantastic, everyone was so friendly and the setting was beautiful, cannot recommend this place enough! ❤️

text: Uno dei tanti parchetti di Venezia. Non è attrezzato, con poche piante e l'ombra del Palazzo della Guardia Di Finanza. C'è però una panchina e non è poco.

text: Originally the private garden of Palazzo Giustinian, this space was redesigned in 1689 by Marco Giustinian, Bishop of Torcello. The garden was restored in the 1990s and is now part of the Murano Glass Museum. It is open to visitors free of charge after museum admission, offering a quiet retreat within the historic grounds.
After spending an hour in the glass museum it was a literal breath of fresh air. I love old trees and to know these were planted here intentionally so long ago...
Minunată grădină!

place_title: Giardino di Palazzo Corner della Ca' Granda
text: Giardino di Palazzo Corner della Ca'Granda shows currently the „peace crystal“ of artist Mariko Mori.

Quiet cosy place to chill when the crowdet venezia is to much for you, and a cool place to watch the ships, cars, people and trains leave rhe city, while having a nice view on the front of the city. Sadly, when we were there, the grass was dry and brown.
Very quiet park
bel parchetto nascosto agli occhi dei più, oserei dire un piccolo locus amoenus

Absolutely lovely hotel.
6 rooms, and a beautiful garden in the middle of Venice.

Located close to everything but not in the touristic area, which makes it even more enjoyable.

The service is outstanding..Oltre il Giardino has definitely become our go to hotel in Venice.

I think this is our third stay over the years having also stayed in the past at The Aman and The Ciptiani - but we return here for the calm personal attention, the location and the good value.||A small suite on the 1st floor looking out over the garden. Sofa and chairs and a large comfortable bed - the rooms minimally decorated adds to the calm open bright feeling. ||Bathroom with bath tub and walk-in shower - all the toiletries you would expect. ||Downstairs breakfast outside on the terrace in the garden and in the colder months in the dining /drawing room. Good selection of breakfast foods, eggs and cold meat to order - freshly (I mean real and just made for you) blood orange juice. Fresh fruits, sweet rolls and breads and fab coffee and maybe just one or two other couples! ||Water taxi transfers to the airport can be arranged.||Local vaporetto at S.Toma - on the 1 and 2 lines. ||Nearby upscale restaurants include Wisteria and Da Fiore plus local trattoria and pizza places places and bars for cicchetti. ||Look out for the cocktail bar Il Mercante just around the corner.

A perfect stay in Venice!  This small hotel, could not be more perfect. Quiet, and convenient local.  Lorenzo, the owner, and his his staff, could not be more friendly and helpful. Warm, and comfortable, and very homey. With only 8 guest rooms, we received so much person attention, and hospitality! Wonderful breakfast, served in the garden, freshly made eggs, perfect fruit, and beautiful pastry. We had a a spacious room, with an extra sitting area. An oasis of serenity, and quiet, in busy Venice! We hated to leave! |Thank you, Lorenzo and staff!

Absolutely love this little hotel. It is a slice of perfection in the heart of Venice. All the staff is lovely. Lorenzo went out of his way to make amazing dinner reservations for us each night of our stay. He also set us up with a fabulous walking tour by a local woman named Rita. Breakfast in the garden was a delicious way to start each day. 5 star experience.

Oltre il Giardino is a lovely hotel option in Venice. The rooms are very comfortable and quiet. The staff are very friendly. They provide really generous breakfasts. ||The hotel is also in a really good location. It’s about a 10 minute walk to the train and bus/taxi stations, 5 minutes to a vaporetto stop, and a nice half-hour stroll to St Mark’s Basillica.

Everything about our stay was an elevated experience. The room, service, and location were all perfect. The staff went above and beyond and assisted us in many ways, including booking water taxis, made multiple reservations, and reccomended and booked an intimate cooking class for us with Sabrina and Jordana.

Spent four wonderful nights here. Perfect location and a quiet oasis after a hard days sight seeing. The room was lovely and the breakfasts were delicious. Pity the weather did not allow us to sit on the terrace. Service was most helpful and efficient. Thank you. We couldn’t have had a better anniversary stay.

Our third visit over the period of 7 years to this wonderful hotel. It feels like a home to us with the friendly team, homely spotless rooms and fantastic breakfast.

The team always greet us in the morning and evening and have a friendly chat with Venice tips and restaurant recommendations.

On this occasion we had our flight cancelled and the hotel were able to offer us a room for an extra night with no stress.

Thank you Oltre Il Giardino, you are a special place to stay!

This square ended up being our favorite thing while visiting Venice. Don't ask why the kids loved it as much as they did, but it gave them something to do for hours while giving us a place to sit in shade and observe the locals.

The pigeon to seagull ratio was perfect, the water fountain gave the chance for the kids to cool themselves down, and there were more locals chilling at the square than tourists. It shouldn't have been as perfect of a place to spend a couple hours once or twice, but it just happened to be the perfect Venezian playground for our kids. Thanks pigeons and the occasional seagull. P.S, one of the locals told us that chasing the seagulls could be a bit dangerous for kids, and they may be right, but our kids did drive the fear of God into the heart of every flying apparatus on this square.

This square has shaded benches, a rarity in Venice. Nice enough place to sit and there is a water fountain to cool off too. We watched a seagull murder and devour a pigeon, which was something of a spectacle.
Don’t go too early nothing to see really
Er ist der schönste Baum in ganz Venedig
Mały placyk z ławkami i gołębiami

This was a beautiful experience. There is a small tour which gives you enough information to inform and make the experience meaningful, but the tour guide allows you to experience the walk and music with yourself. The music composed specifically for the walk through the labyrinth is gorgeous, and walking quietly through while listening is a meditative experience, even if you are not a Borges readers. It was lightly raining, with sun, while we were there which added to the beauty!

We did this as a paired tour with the Vatican Chapels, and together they were an excellent experience.

We would love to experience it in another season, and will probably go back! Thank you!

Know before you go:
-You must book a ticket in advance through the Cini Foundation website.
-This is a symbolic labyrinth, not meant to be like a tourist corn maze or the like, more akin to a meditation garden.
-You should bring your own headphones if you can.

The maze is 1km to walk thru. There is a wonderful story behind the maze that the tour explains.
Booking the tour is the only way to walk through the maze also

Fabulous and mostly undiscovered addition to Venice.  Quick trip from San Zachariah across the bay to the Island.  Reservation for the Labyrinth required (with good reason - too many people would spoil the magic).  No reservation needed for the bell tower, but do bring cash for the elevator ticket.  I think it was 10 euro.
Fantastic place and well deserved homage to the greatest Latin American writer.
Fun experience, get just enough explanation of the labyrinth from the guided tour and musical accompaniment as you walk through. Requires pre-booking

Terrible exeperience! Came
with two children to only visit the labyrinth and it was already fully booked. If you want to visit, book the visit earlier (1-2 days prior)!!
You're not able to visit the labyrinth by yourself, only with a guided tour during certain hours of the day, which you have to book in advance.
The labyrinth is nice but if you want to see and visit it you have to pay 14 euro: too much for a audio registered audio guide. Not worth the money.

--Atmosphere--
The atmosphere is great. The caffé is in a really lovely and gorgeous garden with an old but gold building. The whole building gives a vintage and cozy house feeling. You should definitely visit here if you have the time.

--Service--
Service was good and I liked it but I give 3 stars instead of 4 because they don't allow you to split the bill equally. Surely it is their choice if we are allowed to split the bill equally among customers. However it is my choice to deduct points from this lack of choice regarding the payment process. Other than that the staff was friendly and the service had no delays.

--Food--
I had just the right amount of coffee with my americano with milk on the side, however the amount of coffee was vague regarding their special recipes so my friends looking for some caffeine didn't feel like they had less coffee than what they wanted.

Caffé La Serra was such a beautiful spot for a late breakfast. The setting is quiet and peaceful, with the café nestled right next to a historic greenhouse filled with gorgeous plants. Sitting outside was refreshing and relaxing—a perfect way to start the day.

We enjoyed delicious paninis, great coffee, and brioche, all in a charming atmosphere. After your food or drinks, definitely take time to explore the greenhouse. Highly recommend this little gem if you’re in Giardini.

A cafe in one of the oldest buildings on the island that's part garden too? I'm intrigued. It's a very charming space with many opportunities for photos. The space is the highlight and main reason to check it out. As mentioned in other reviews, the food and coffee are decent/ok, and the service is not up to caliber. Though  still worth going at least once.

Not very friendly staff, pigeons flying into the indoors which is terrible, but probably the best cafe option around the Giardinni Biennale area that’s of reasonable price and with a range of light food options, also pretty nice botanical environment (if there would not be pigeons!)

Tucked away in the heart of the Venice gardens, this charming café offered a delightful escape from the bustling crowds. We enjoyed their excellent coffee and a perfectly made panini, making for a simple yet satisfying meal. The serene setting allowed us to experience a quieter, more tranquil side of Venice—a refreshing change from the usual tourist spots. Highly recommend this spot for anyone looking to enjoy great food in a peaceful atmosphere!

We only had coffee. The place is amazing, such a gem, far away from the crowd and the touristy area. Coffee was ok. We found it by chance, and it was really nice to sit there and enjoy a coffee with so much greenery around. But the service is bad and the toilet is terrible in terms of cleanliness.
Great atmosphere and view, lovely music, very peaceful and cozy 💖🌻

I went there 1 year ago. The food is just okay you can find other good choice nearby. And then they refuse me for one cappuccino, a very tipical breakfast, this year. This restaurant is just for nice Instagram photos not food.👆👎🤌🤌🤌

Our stay with Marco made our trip to Venice. I've never had such a caring, thoughtful and kind service in a B and B.|| Marco's breakfast is delicious and we could stay and chat all day! We were so grateful for Marco's knowledge and tips for navigating Venice.|| The B and B is perfect, so many lovely touches (including Marcos delicious home baked bread and cakes!) , perfect cleanliness and so comfortable. Within walking distance of everything you could need. We would love to come and stay again please Marco! X

Marco runs this 3-room B&B and shows his love for what he does every day. The breakfast he serves is superb and a chance to get to know Marco and his other guests. Marco is one of the nicest and most personable people I have ever had the pleasure of meeting. Rooms are very clean and comfortable, and the B&B is in a great location. Walking to and from the train station takes just a few minutes, and yet you are in the heart of Venice when you walk out the door. There is nothing missing here and the price was very good. Good restaurants, bars and Gelato are just steps away. I will definitely stay here again if I should ever get back to Venice.

We came to stay with Marco in July 2020, sadly in some ways we were the only guests, but while in Venice it was amazing as we felt like we almost had the city to ourselves.||||Marco has taken every precaution and care to help visitors stay safe during Covid, which we really appreciated.||||We stayed for 3 nights, feeling like we should have stayed a few more. Sadly Marcos reputed amazing breakfast was not available due to the Covid rules, but Marco introduced us to the café which sites directly opposite. In fact staying here you feel like you are in the real Venice, away from the tourist traps and welcomed into the venetian family. There are also 3 restaurants within 200 metres which can offer you anything from basic to really authentic venetian food. Marco can also recommend where you can get fantastic Ventian cicchetti (Tapas)...||||Marco is a superb host, an original venetian (there are few left) who is happy to give you amazing hints and tips that will make a real difference to your stay. ||||The rooms are all individually decorated and clean and have good ventilation in case of warm weather, which we had.

Great location (very close to train and bus station as well as vaporetto stop). Il Giardino Di Giulia, a beautiful old listed house with great views. The host Marco is super helpful and prepares delicious homemade breakfast. Thank you Marco! We will be back!

I recently stayed six nights with Marco. I chose his place because of its very convenient location near the railway and bus station and because Santa Croce is less crowded with tourists than other parts of Venice. The rooms are very spacious and mine looked out onto a historic church courtyard. There is a good library of books about Venice in the hallway. I found Marco to be one of the most interesting people I have met and extremely obliging with information, advice and practical assistance with travellers’ needs. Marco cooks a great breakfast, (including pancakes made to a secret Irish recipe) in addition to providing a wide range of cold smorgasbord options. Stimulating conversations with Marco can be conducted in either Italian, English or French. I would certainly return and recommend Marco’s place to other travellers.

Marco is a very nice host, he provided me with useful information and recommendations for sightseeing and dinner. My room was on the ground floor, bigger than I expected, nice view to the church garden, antique furniture and very nice decorated with nautical objects. Every morning I chatted with Marco during an excellent breakfast with delicious home-made pancakes which was the perfect start of the day. The palazzo is close to the bus station (good when I arrived from airport), near the train station (for my trip to Padua), very close to Vaporetto stop (for sightseeing on the canal with line 1 or 2, trips to Lido, Murano and Burano) and in a very nice neighborhood with many restaurants and bars where also locals go. ||I really enjoyed my time there and definitely recommend the place!

My wife and I stayed at Marco's place for four nights. Marco is the perfect host! He speaks fluent English (and French too) and told us the best places to eat and explained where to get the vaporetto from, for our planned excursion the following day. He made us delicious pancakes for breakfast, with excellent coffee and homemade cakes on offer too.||The property is within walking distance of both Santa Lucia train station and Piazzale Roma bus station (approximately 5 and 10 mins respectively). This might not sound like a big deal, but if you're arriving by train or bus in the middle of summer, you won't want to be walking far with luggage or paying for an expensive water taxi. Despite being located in a busy 'Calle', the rooms are quiet and as most tourists have left by nightfall, there is little in the way of noise.||We will definitely be returning and can't wait to catch up with Marco again!

I stayed 3 nights at Marco‘s BnB. Even the booking procedure and first exchange of E-mails was already very welcoming.|The BnB is just 5 minutes walking distance from the train station which means, you can get there before getting annoyed by the crowds ;-)|Marco was seemingly already waiting for me and directly opened the door while I was still wondering whether I was standing in front of the right building. This charming host made my stay in Venice unforgettable with his stories and recommendations. The BnB consists of 3 rooms which are all nicely decorated and way more spacious than I would have assumed for a small palazzo from the 16th century. The breakfast is great and the coffee brought me every morning back to life ;-)|Also the location of the BnB is perfect as you‘re in walking distance to main sites as well as to bars and restaurants without being directly in the center of the tourist hordes. I wouldn‘t want to stay anywhere else when I‘ll return to Venice!
Unica villa di Venezia
Perfect place to chill and read after a little walk!
Not crowded place, there are a few tourists.
An area for the intrepid who wants to experience Venice in less crowded quarters.
I spend in this hotel 2 nights. Very good for short time. Close to city centre, clean. Breakfast nothing special
The Last island in Venice, seat of the Venice Cathedral (not to be confused with St.Mark's Basilica).
You may get a whole different sensation of Venice if you come up to here.
Quiet and peaceful. Relax away from the crowds.

Die Mönche stellen auch verschiedene Heilmittel aus Melissa her, die in der Klosterapotheke käuflich erworben werden können!

A calm and restful green space in this area. It is well shaded with trees, with grassy areas for sitting or playing. There are plenty of benches to sit on for rest. On the edge of this Campo there is a very good Osteria, and a small grocery and bread shop, and a butcher shop that makes sandwiches. Nearby is an excellent fruit and vegetable shop. It is quiet here and somewhere the residents of this place come out to in the evening.
Calm and peaceful area in Venice!

I would like to express my deepest gratitude to my friend Vera for planning a walk to this cafe for us❣️This is how I imagined my dream cafe.. what windows there are!! My friends and I had a great time there, some of us even managed to take a nap. Also, appreciate the sound of the cicadas, you can hear them in the video.

I’ve recently came across this spectacular cafe in Venice. It sure has a very different vibe and atmosphere from all the other bars in Venice. It’s located in a small beautiful garden and they also sell plants. The plants, high roof, and the glass windows make it a very pretty place for relaxing and enjoying your day. We tried the hot chocolate with cream which I really liked, and the sweets (cupcake and small cornetto) tasted good too. The prices are about 1€ higher that other bars in Venice.

Having only 1 waitress to take order AND pay the bills and only 1 waitress to clean the tables in a busy Sunday is not a good idea. Visited this place on the 19th of June 2024. Decent prices, decent drinks. I've visited it twice on the same day, at lunchtime, trying to find some shade, and once later the afternoon while my spose was visiting the Giardini della Bienalle . The shade was not the best, I can't really say that umbrellas provide the best shade, but the drinks were awesome (had some americano coffees, a machiato, some sprits) . Plus points for the venue, a former greenhouse dating back to 1890s.
